content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      :    File                                           Date  :  Sept. 14, 1983
                                                                    No. T-83-206


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Janis Shank McLean

Subject:   Telephone Advice to Raymond Simmons


         Mr. Simmons' letter asked about possible Political Reform
Act violations by his political opponents. I said that I
couldn't advise him, but I reminded him that the Act focuses on
decision making by officials (see his letter). I sent him an
enforcement complaint form because this matter involved past
acts.

         He is the official described in the Little League
situation. I told him that nothing stops him from being school
superintendent and the director of the Little League. Section
87103(d) doesn't prevent his decision making on matters affecting
the Little League because it is nonprofit.

JSM:plh

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660              322-5901           322-6441            322-6444


September 8, 1983


Raymond J. Simmons
Chief Elections Clerk
El Dorado County
360 Fair Lane
Placerville, CA 95667
                                                Re: A-83-206

Dear Mr. Simmons:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        /Chief, Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

DOLORES BREDESON
COUNTY CLERK
ELECTIONS DEPT.
360 Fair Lane
Placerville, CA 95667
Telephone: (916) 626-2266

EL DORADO COUNTY

August 29, 1983

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Attn:  Legal/Technical Assistance

Dear Sir/Madam:

     In anticipation of an issue which may arise in one of our school
district elections I submit for your discussion and opinion the following
circumstances and questions.

     CONCERN:  Possible violation of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform
Act of 1974.

     A school district in the process of building a new school let a
contract which was awarded to a construction firm, which in turn sub-
contracted the clearing and grubbing of brush and trees to a local
contractor.  Under the authority of the contract, the subcontractor had
the right to permit anyone to clear and grub.

     A trustee of the District, because of his occupation, had knowledge
of the availability of the clearing and grubbing several months prior to
his appointment as a trustee.  He contacted the subcontractor as an in-
dependent woodcutter, not as a trustee, and obtained permission to remove
the wood from the school site.

     He was joined in the removal and sharing of the wood by another
trustee and two other individuals.  Removal of the wood took place over
a three month period.  During this time the District Superintendent became
aware of the dealings but declined to take any type of action.

     ISSUES:

     1.  Was there any action by the independent woodcutter/trustee which
would be violative of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974?

2. Was there any action by the second trustee which would be
violative of the Act?

3. Assuming arguendo that one of the individuals who assisted in
the removal of the wood now stored said wood at his residence for future
sale. Assuming further that the individual declares his candidacy for
election as a trustee of the school district and subsequently is elected.
Would the future sale of the wood be violative of the Act?

CONCERN: Possible violation of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform
Act of 1974.

A school district superintendent is also the treasurer and a member
of the board of directors of a local nonprofit corporation, specifically
Little League.

A member of the same little league board of directors is also a
candidate for the school board of the district which employs the super-
intendent.

The local little league regularly enters into written and oral
contracts with the school district regarding use and maintenance of base-
ball diamonds which have been constructed on school grounds with little
league funds.

ISSUES:

1. May the superintendent acting as a director of the little league
vote on matters which require the expenditure of funds paid for improvements
to school property in the form of baseball diamonds, fencing, bleachers and
snackbars which the school also uses for its students?

2. May the superintendent enter into contracts, while acting as
superintendent, with the local little league which requires approval of
the little league board of directors?

3. If the little league director who is a school board candidate is
elected to the school board, may he retain both positions? When must he
abstain from voting on a matter?

Different interpretations may be given to section 87103 (d) of the
Act which may or may not permit the individuals heretofore mentioned to
participate in both little league and school board decisions which have
reciprocal effects.

Your prompt attention and reply will be greatly appreciated.

Sincerely,

Raymond J. Simmons
Chief Elections Clerk

sections.conclusion: Section 87103(d) doesn't prevent his decision making on matters affecting the Little League because it is nonprofit. Nothing stops him from being school superintendent and the director of the Little League.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a school superintendent who serves as treasurer and board member of a local nonprofit Little League vote on school district matters affecting the Little League, and if a Little League board member is elected to the school board, may he retain both positions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Section 87103(d) of the Political Reform Act does not prohibit a superintendent from serving simultaneously as a Little League director because the Little League is a nonprofit organization, and there is no legal bar to holding both positions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request letter from county clerk (August 29, 1983) and FPPC's internal memorandum response (September 14, 1983). The memorandum is brief and does not contain formal Q&A sections. Question and conclusion marked as null because they are not verbatim section headers, but synthetic versions provided based on document content. Lower confidence due to informal nature of internal memo and limited detail in FPPC's stated conclusion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Can a school superintendent who serves as treasurer and board member of a local nonprofit Little League vote on school district matters affecting the Little League, and if a Little League board member is elected to the school board, may he retain both positions?

CONCLUSION: Section 87103(d) doesn't prevent his decision making on matters affecting the Little League because it is nonprofit. Nothing stops him from being school superintendent and the director of the Little League.

embedding.summary: FPPC internal memorandum documenting telephone advice given to Raymond Simmons regarding whether a school superintendent can serve as director of a local nonprofit Little League and participate in related decisions. The FPPC advised that Section 87103(d) permits such dual service because the Little League is nonprofit.
